text: I found the birthday card my daughter made me two weeks ago, still unopened. It was buried under a stack of mail I had not sorted. The glitter was smudged and she had drawn a heart on the envelope. She must have watched me walk past it every day. I opened it now, alone in the kitchen, and her wobbly handwriting said I love you Dad. My stomach turned into a stone.

I told my best friend I was busy when she called crying. I was not busy. I was watching television. I just did not want to deal with it. An hour later she texted saying she was okay. I stared at the message and something sour settled in my chest. I kept replaying the moment I declined the call. I could have picked up. I should have picked up.

I ate the last piece of my roommate's cake. She had been saving it, had even written her name on the container. I ate it standing over the sink at midnight and threw away the container. The next morning she opened the fridge and I watched her face fall. She did not say anything. I did not either. The silence between us felt like a splinter under my skin.

I snapped at my mother on the phone. She was just asking about my day and I told her I did not have time for this. The line went quiet. She said okay, honey, and hung up. I sat there holding the phone. She calls because she is lonely. I know this. I put the phone down and pressed my palms into my eyes. The sting behind them was not from tiredness.

I backed into a parked car at the grocery store. I looked around and no one was watching. I pulled forward, checked my bumper, and drove away. But all the way home I kept seeing the dent in my mirror. I thought about the person coming out with their bags, finding the damage, looking around the lot. I went back an hour later but the car was gone.

I promised my son I would come to his recital and I missed it. A work call ran long and I told myself I would make the second half. I did not. When I got home he was already in his pajamas. He said it was fine. He said this looking at the floor. I knelt down and hugged him and felt something crack open in my chest that I could not close.

I gossiped about a friend behind her back. I told a story that made her look foolish because it got a laugh. Later that night I lay in bed replaying it. The way people laughed. The way I had leaned in, enjoying the attention. She trusts me. She tells me things because she thinks they are safe with me. My stomach churned and I stared at the ceiling until two in the morning.

I found the dog trembling under the table and realized I had forgotten to let him out before my eight-hour shift. There was a puddle by the door. He looked at me with those brown eyes and his tail gave one tentative wag. I cleaned the floor and gave him treats but the weight in my chest would not lift. He had been waiting all day and I just forgot.

I read the text I sent last night while drunk. It was mean and specific and aimed at exactly the places I knew would hurt. I read it again in the morning light and my face burned. I could not unsend it. I could not unread it. I typed sorry and erased it. Typed it again. Erased it again. Nothing I could say was proportional to what I had done.

I voted against hiring the candidate because I did not like her personally. She was the most qualified. Everyone said so. But I gave my feedback wrapped in professional language about culture fit, and the committee passed on her. I watched her leave the building from the window. She straightened her blazer and walked to her car. I sat back down and the chair felt hard.

I did not visit my grandmother in the hospital. I kept saying I would go tomorrow. Tomorrow turned into next week. Next week turned into three weeks. She died on a Tuesday while I was at brunch. At the funeral, my cousin said she had been asking for me. I stood at the casket and the flowers smelled too sweet and my legs felt like they might buckle.

I took credit for the idea in the meeting. It was actually my intern's suggestion from a conversation we had over coffee. She was right there in the room. I saw her blink when I said it. She did not correct me. After the meeting I walked past her desk and she was typing with her jaw set tight. I kept walking. The idea was already mine in everyone's mind.

I threw away my kid's drawing to make room on the fridge. It was just a scribble, I told myself. But that evening she ran to the kitchen to show her aunt and the space was empty. She looked at me with a question on her face. I said it must have fallen behind the fridge. I watched her get on her hands and knees to look for it and something clenched in my throat.

I lied on my resume. Not a big lie -- I said I managed a team of eight when it was really four. But they hired me based partly on that experience. Every time my boss praises my leadership, I feel a small twist behind my ribs. I keep waiting for someone to check. The lie is a pebble in my shoe that I cannot shake out.

I saw an elderly man struggling with his groceries in the rain. I was warm in my car. I watched him fumble with the bags and drop a carton of eggs. He bent down slowly, stiffly. I could have helped. I had time. But I put the car in drive and pulled away. A block later I caught his reflection in the side mirror, still bending in the rain.

I forgot my wedding anniversary. My wife did not. She had reservations, a gift, a card she must have written that morning. I came home talking about a bad day at work and she was in a new dress. I saw the card on the table and the date hit me like cold water. She smiled and said it was okay. The way she said okay made my insides twist.

I returned the shirt I had worn to a party. I wore it once, washed it carefully, reattached the tags, and brought it back for a full refund. The cashier did not question it. I walked out with the money and felt a coldness settle in my stomach. It was thirty dollars. It should not have mattered. But I kept thinking about the store clerk inspecting it after I left.

I deleted the voicemail without listening to it. It was from my dad and I knew it would be long and rambling and I did not want to deal with it. A week later my sister told me he had been calling to tell me about his diagnosis. He wanted me to hear it from him. I sat with my phone in my hand and felt the weight of a small, unforgivable laziness.

I watched my colleague get reprimanded for a mistake I made. She did not know it was me who entered the wrong numbers. She apologized to the boss with red eyes and I sat two desks away saying nothing. The spreadsheet was still open on my screen. I closed it quietly. For the rest of the day I could not meet her eyes.

I told my friend I liked her haircut. I did not. She had been anxious about it all week. She looked at me with such relief and said really? and I said yes, it looks great. It was a kindness. But also it was not the truth. She made three more appointments based on my opinion and each time I saw her I felt a small pull in my stomach.

I accidentally ran over my neighbor's cat. I felt the bump and stopped and found it in the road. It was already gone. I carried it to their porch and left. I could not ring the bell. I could not face the children. I went home and washed my hands three times and sat on the couch and could still feel the bump in my palms.

I broke my sister's necklace, the one our mother gave her before she died. I was borrowing it without asking. The clasp snapped and the beads scattered across the bathroom floor. I collected them all and put them in an envelope. I still have not told her. The envelope sits in my drawer and every time I open it I feel the same sick pull in my stomach.

I cheated on the exam. I had the answers on my phone under the desk. I passed with the highest grade. The professor shook my hand and said he was impressed. I smiled and thanked him. That night I lay awake and the grade felt like a mark burned into my skin. The diploma on the wall would always have an asterisk only I could see.

I canceled plans with my grieving friend three times in a row. Each excuse was reasonable on its own. Work, fatigue, a headache. But the pattern was clear and I knew it. She stopped asking. I saw her post online that she felt alone and I recognized my handprints on that loneliness. I picked up the phone. Put it down. Picked it up again.

I yelled at my toddler for spilling juice on the rug. It was an accident and she was trying to pour it herself, something I encouraged. She froze and her lip trembled and then she cried. I had made that face happen. I knelt down and held her, saying sorry over and over. She forgave me in seconds. I did not forgive myself for the rest of the day.
